coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of procalcitonin using dual-color carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes in FRET assay
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yuxin
Du, Yuwei
Chen, Zhang
Li, Li
Yan, Ruhong
Li, Xiaoqiang
Yue, Juan
coredata.dc:description: FRET, known as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer, serves as a versatile technique enabling precise quantitative assessment of protein concentrations with minimal disruption to biological processes. In this study, we have developed two types of fluorescent carbon quantum dots, B-CDs and G-CDs, serving as donor and acceptor probes, respectively, for FRET-based procalcitonin (PCT) detection. The B-CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence (excitation/emission peaks at 360/442 nm) as donors, while the G-CDs display faint green fluorescence (excitation/emission peaks at 449/520 nm) as acceptors. These CDs are conjugated with PCT antibodies to form immune probes. Upon antigen binding, the antibody quickly binds and reacts with the antigen. Concurrently, the fluorescence energy from the donor is transferred to the acceptor, leading to a decrease in the blue fluorescence intensity emitted by B-CDs. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity between the green probe (G-CDs-Ab2) and the blue probe (B-CDs-Ab1) exhibited a positive correlation with the concentration of PCT antigen in the low concentration range of 0.5 to 5 ng/mL. Consistent results were observed in both phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and diluted human serum. In the concentration range of 0.5–5 ng/mL in PBS buffer, the linear equation is expressed as F A /F D = −0.0045 × c [PCT]^2 + 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.3232, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ng/mL based on 3σ K−1. For concentrations spanning from 5-80 ng/mL, the equation is F A /F D = 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.4212. These findings suggest that carbon dot-labeled immune probes could offer a novel approach for sensitive detection of disease biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FRET
Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Procalcitonin
Rapid detection
Dual-color

Reagent synthesis of immunoprobes conjugated with carbon dots (CDs)
Detection of pH in B-CDs and G-CDs
Detection of procalcitonin (PCT) in the PBS buffer
Detection of low concentration PCT in diluted serum
Characterization of the B-CDs and G-CDs
Detection of pH in B-CDs and G-CDs
Identification of procalcitonin (PCT) in the PBS solution
Interference test of serum substances on the detection of PCT
Detection of low-concentration PCT in blood serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of B-CDs and G-CDs, including (a-b) high-resolution TEM images along with nanoparticle size distributions, (c) FTIR spectra, and (d) UV–vis absorption spectra with a partially enlarged view for enhanced visualization in the upper right corner of the image.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs within the excited wavelength from 350 nm to 400 nm, and (b) G-CDs within the excited wavelength from 440 nm to 480 nm. (c) Combined plot of the best emission spectra for B-CDs and G-CDs, and (d) the combination of the emission spectrum of B-CDs and the excitation spectrum of G-CDs.

(a) Under daylight and (b) UV light illumination, B-CDs and G-CDs from left to right correspond to solution pH ranging from 8.0 to 2.0. (c) Correspond to the emission spectra of B-CDs and (d) G-CDs in solutions with different pH values. (e) Schematic illustration of the impact of substances in human serum on the fluorescence intensity of donors and recipients.
The TEM image of (a) B-CDs-Ab1 and (b) G-CDs-Ab2, (c) reveal the FTIR spectrum between (c) B-CDs (blue line) and B-CDs-Ab1 (red line), (d) G-CDs (green line) and B-CDs-Ab1 (red line).

(a) (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs and corresponding conjugated antibodies (as the 310 nm the excitation wavelength) and (c), (d) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and corresponding conjugated antibodies (as the 410 nm the excitation wavelength).

(a) The fluorescence emission spectrum of (a) B-CDs-Ab1 probe when interacting with varying concentrations of PCT antigen in a PBS buffer at pH = 7.4; (b) the linear relationship between PCT antigen concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio from dual probes in PBS buffer solution; (c) the fluorescence emission spectra for both B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 probes upon interaction with different concentrations of PCT antigen in human serum; and (d) the linear correlation between PCT antigen concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio from these two probes in human serum; (e) plot of fluorescence lifetime changes in reaction of donor probe with antigen and acceptor probe with antigen.

(a) The synthetic pathway of the donor (B-CDs) and acceptor (G-CDs) (detail in the Supporting Information); (b) the conjugation with anti-procalcitonin Ab1 and Ab2 forming fluorescent probe B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2, respectively; (c) the schematic illustration of the principle of PCT detection utilizing a dual-fluorescent probe.
The quantum yield, fluorescence lifetime, and excitation/emission wavelength of donor–acceptor carbon dots in water or PBS solution.

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of procalcitonin using dual-color carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes in FRET assay

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: FRET, known as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer, serves as a versatile technique enabling precise quantitative assessment of protein concentrations with minimal disruption to biological processes. In this study, we have developed two types of fluorescent carbon quantum dots, B-CDs and G-CDs, serving as donor and acceptor probes, respectively, for FRET-based procalcitonin (PCT) detection. The B-CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence (excitation/emission peaks at 360/442 nm) as donors, while the G-CDs display faint green fluorescence (excitation/emission peaks at 449/520 nm) as acceptors. These CDs are conjugated with PCT antibodies to form immune probes. Upon antigen binding, the antibody quickly binds and reacts with the antigen. Concurrently, the fluorescence energy from the donor is transferred to the acceptor, leading to a decrease in the blue fluorescence intensity emitted by B-CDs. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity between the green probe (G-CDs-Ab2) and the blue probe (B-CDs-Ab1) exhibited a positive correlation with the concentration of PCT antigen in the low concentration range of 0.5 to 5 ng/mL. Consistent results were observed in both phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and diluted human serum. In the concentration range of 0.5–5 ng/mL in PBS buffer, the linear equation is expressed as F A /F D = −0.0045 × c [PCT]^2 + 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.3232, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ng/mL based on 3σ K−1. For concentrations spanning from 5-80 ng/mL, the equation is F A /F D = 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.4212. These findings suggest that carbon dot-labeled immune probes could offer a novel approach for sensitive detection of disease biomarkers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomarkers, either individually or in combination, offer high specificity and sensitivity in disease diagnosis [1,2]. Procalcitonin (PCT) functions as a critical biomarker for the early diagnosis of infections and postoperative evaluations. Its rapid and dynamic response profile renders it especially valuable for guiding antibiotic therapy in clinical practice [3]. Monitoring changes in PCT levels enables the rapid quantification of infection severity, aiding in the timely selection of modification of the most suitable antibiotic therapy [4]. In recent years, there has been a growing acknowledgment that PCT not only possesses diagnostic value for infection and sepsis but also serves as an effective tool for monitoring the treatment of various other diseases. For example, Dr. Zeng et al. reported that hepatocellular carcinoma cells can secrete PCT even in the absence of infection, indicating the potential utility of PCT as a biomarker for assessing tumor treatment efficacy [5]. Furthermore, PCT has also been identified as a valuable biomarker in the context of cardiac surgery [6]. Despite notable progress in clinical immunoassays for PCT detection, the sensitive and precise detection, along with dynamic monitoring during the initial phases of treatment, remains a significant challenge [7].

Currently, PCT immunoassay methods include chemiluminescence, immunofluorescence, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), immunoturbidimetry, radioimmunoassay, and colloidal gold colorimetry [8–11]. These platforms exhibit notable variations in technical attributes, including sensitivity, processing time, and detection limits. ELISA, characterized by its complexity, introduces numerous potential interfering factors [12]. The specificity and sensitivity of chemiluminescence combined with antigen–antibody immune response are very suitable for clinical detection [13–15]. Nevertheless, due to the short fluorescence time of the luminescent substance, the challenge of background elevation still has certain constraints on detection sensitivity. Immunofluorescence is a well-established quantitative immunodetection technique, wherein the utilization of fluorescein as a tracer does not impede the antigen–antibody binding process. Typically, organic small-molecule fluorescent dyes are employed for immunofluorescence labeling [16]. The utilization of organic small-molecule fluorophores for labeling serves to enhance the fluorescence signal and enhance sensitivity, thereby mitigating potential delays in the procedure [17,18]. However, small organic dyes exhibit limitations in immunofluorescence labeling owing to their insufficient photochemical stability, significant photodegradation, and limited fluorescence lifetime [19]. Therefore, it is imperative to continue investigating innovative materials with superior characteristics to enable efficient PCT detection at this stage.

As an emerging category of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) possess ideal biosensor properties owing to their distinctive optical characteristics, exceptional stability, aqueous solubility, minimal toxicity, and remarkable biocompatibility [20–22]. The distinctive photophysical and photochemical properties of CDs render them exceptionally suitable for detecting biomarkers and facilitating cell imaging in various applications [23–26]. Such as Dou et al. developed a ratio-metric fluorescent NMOFs-APTA sensor for the quantitative determination of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) by integrating Cy3-modified aptamers with zirconium-based nano-metal–organic frameworks (NMOFs). The fluorescence of the NMOFs-Aptasensor was modulated through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), allowing for the quantitative determination of AFB1 by analyzing the fluorescence signal ratios [27]. Therefore, carbon quantum dots exhibiting a high quantum yield have the potential to supplant conventional fluorophores in labeling tasks. The FRET method ensures not only heightened sensitivity and specificity in immunofluorescence but also facilitates quantitative detection by leveraging the ratio of donor and acceptor fluorescence signals. Furthermore, the influence of fluorescence attenuation on detection outcomes is notably diminished [28–30].

In this study, donor–acceptor dual carbon dots were designed and synthesized following the FRET principles. By integrating the high specificity and sensitivity inherent to the double-antibody sandwich assay, a dual-fluorescent ratio-metric probe adept at recognizing PCT antigen was successfully formulated. This innovation facilitates the swift and precise quantification of PCT levels (as illustrated in Scheme 1 ). The antibody functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots were activated via classical NHS/EDC chemistry, thereby enabling efficient construction of the immunoprobe [31]. The applicability of this approach was validated by the detection of PCT in both PBS solution and human serum.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, meta-phenylenediamine, 0.1 M PBS buffer, methanol, dichloromethane, silica gel, sand, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), silicon dioxide, and bovine serum albumin were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Passiflora edulis Sims shell powder was purchased from Shanxi Yongyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. PCT monoclonal antibody pairs and PCT (murine-derived Human IgG) were provided by Beijing Bioss Biological Co., Ltd. Ultra-pure water was produced utilizing the ultra-pure water system at the Academy of Military Medical Sciences. All reagents were used in their original packaging without requiring addition purification, ensuring analytical purity.

The synthesized carbon dots (B-CDs/G-CDs) was characterized by various physicochemical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Transmittance electron microscopy (HRTEM), Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscop. The infrared spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs were analyzed by Nicolet iS20 infrared spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific in the United States. The sample was powdered using an agate mortar, followed by thorough drying in an 80 °C oven. A portion of the sample was then mixed with KBr in a mortar at a ratio of 1:100 and subsequently compressed into tablets using a stainless steel die. The spectral absorption of the sample was observed in the spectrometer with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scanning range of 4000–400 cm−1.

The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and G-CDs were analyzed by F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer. Both types of carbon dots were prepared as 1 mg/mL aqueous solutions. B-CDs were excited in the range of 350–400 nm, and the emission wavelengths under different excitation modes were recorded. G-CDs were excited in the range of 440–480 nm following the same procedure. XRD measurements were carried out using a Rigaku SmartLab SE of Nippon Science Corporation diffractometer with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA, and the data were recorded in the range from 10 to 90° (2θ). TEM images were performed with an JEOL JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope with an operating accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded from 190 to 1100 nm using an UL-5000 Ultra-microspectrophotometer. The absolute quantum yield and fluorescence lifespan of the B-CDs, G-CDs, B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 were measuring using a FLS1000 equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of blue luminescent carbon dots (B-CDs) was accomplished by modifying a previously established method [31]. Ethylenediamine-passivated fluorescent carbon dots were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal process. The procedure commenced by dissolving 1.051 g of citric acid in 15 mL of deionized water, followed by the subsequent addition of 335 μL of ethylenediamine. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the liquid was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove any undissolved materials. The resulting crude product was combined with silica gel, and the coarse powder was subjected to rotary evaporation with methanol and dichloromethane as eluents in a gradient starting from a 1:10 ratio. The eluted solution was then mixed with silica gel and underwent a second round of rotary evaporation to produce a bright yellow powder, which was store in a light-protected refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The synthesis of green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) were performed with modifications based on existing literature [32]. A mixture of 0.5 g Passiflora edulis Sims shell powder and 0.8 g meta-phenylenediamine was prepared in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then stirred and subjected to ultrasonication before being transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 160 °C for 8 h. Following this, the resulting brown liquid underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove any undissolved materials. The subsequent purification steps followed the same procedure used for B-CDs. Finally, the yellowish powder obtained from spin evaporation was preserved in a light-shielded refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reagent synthesis of immunoprobes conjugated with carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The carboxyl groups on the surface of B-CDs and G-CDs were initially activated using EDC/NHS. The trapping antibody (Ab1) and the detecting antibody (Ab2) were covalently conjugated to the donor (B-CDs) and the receptor (G-CDs), respectively. The procedure was as follows: B-CDs powder was dissolved in 0.01 M PBS buffer (2.5 mg/mL) and filtered to obtain a pure B-CDs solution. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.5–7.5 to facilitate antibody coupling. After that, 20 mg of EDC and 10 mg of NHS were added to 1 mL of the carboxylic acid-modified B-CDs solution and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min on a shaking table. Subsequently, 25 μL of antibody solution Ab1 (0.5 mg/mL in 0.01 M PBS) was introduced and incubated at room temperature in a shaker for 4 h, followed by an additional 20 h at 4 °C to allow covalent coupling of the antibody with the carbon dots. Finally, 300 μL of the solution was transferred to a Millipore 3 K 0.5 mL ultrafiltration centrifugal tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 45 min. The concentrated solution was then washed with 300 μL of PBS buffer to remove any unbound carbon dots, EDC, and NHS. The resultant B-CDs-Ab1 solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments, in accordance with standard laboratory protocols. G-CDs-Ab2 were synthesized following the identical procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pH in B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Buffers with various pH values ranging from 2.0 to 8.0 was prepared by mixing 0.2 M disodium hydrogen phosphate solution and 0.1 M citric acid solution in different proportions. Solutions containing 50 μg/mL of B-CDs and G-CDs was prepared. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and G-CDs at different pH levels were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelengths set at 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of procalcitonin (PCT) in the PBS buffer
ce:section.ce:para: PCT antigen was diluted to concentrations of 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 ng/mL using an antigen–antibody diluent (0.01 M PBS containing 3 % BSA). Subsequently, 10 μL of B-CD-Ab1 and 10 μL of G-CD-Ab2 were added to 180 μL of the same diluent. Varying concentrations of PCT (100 μL) were introduced and incubated with B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 at 37 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence intensities of the B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 probes were measured with excitation wavelength at 360 nm and 450 nm, and PCT intensity denoted as FD and FA, respectively. Consequently, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio FA/FD and PCT concentration was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of low concentration PCT in diluted serum
ce:section.ce:para: PCT antigen was diluted to concentrations of 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5 ng/mL with antigen–antibody diluent (0.01 M PBS, containing 3 % BSA solution), respectively. Blood serum samples were diluted 500-fold with the same antigen–antibody diluent. To each 180 μL of the diluted blood serum, 10 μL each of B-CD-Ab1 and G-CD-Ab2 were added, followed by the addition of 100 μL PCT antigen at various concentrations. The aforementioned mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min and the fluorescence emitted by B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 probes was quantified with excitation wavelength at 360 nm and 450 nm. The corresponding PCT intensity were recorded as FD and FA, respectively. The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio FA/FD and PCT concentration was subsequently determined.

FRET relies on the photophysical processes associated with the distance between donor and acceptor molecules. In this process, excited fluorophores transfer energy to adjacent acceptor molecules through nonradiative transitions mediated by inter-dipole interactions. Consequently, this results in the quenching of donor fluorescence and the augmentation of acceptor fluorescence [33]. According to Eq. (1), the FRET efficiency is contingent upon the distance r separating the donor and acceptor, as well as the Forster distance R0 [34].
(1)
E =
1
1 +
r
R 0
6
(2)
R
0
6
=
9 ∙
ln 10
∙
κ
2
∙
Q D
∙
ε A
∙ J
λ
128 ∙
π
5
∙
N A
∙
n
4

In summary, we have successfully engineered a highly sensitive carbon dot-based probe system for immunoassays. As depicted in Scheme 1(b), the commercially available PCT monoclonal antibodies Ab1 and Ab2 were covalently conjugated to the surfaces of B-CDs and G-CDs, respectively, through NHS/EDC activation. This process yielded results donor probes (B-CDs-Ab1) and acceptor probes (G-CDs-Ab2). In the absence of PCT antigen, no significant interaction was observed between B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2. However, with increasing concentration of PCT antigen, its binding with both donor and recipient probes activated the immune “sandwich” detection system, resulting in a marked alteration in the fluorescence emitted by the dual-probe complex. Upon excitation at 360 nm, donor probe emitted intense blue light. The binding of PCT antigen to B-CDs-Ab1 facilitated efficient energy transfer to G-CDs-Ab2, resulting in enhanced photoluminescence of G-CDs-Ab2 and a gradual decrease in the luminescence intensity of B-CDs-Ab1, exhibiting a direct correlation with PCT concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological attributes of B-CDs and G-CDs were investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), B-CDs manifested a dispersed and homogeneous configuration, resembling spherical particles with sizes ranging from 2 to 9 nm. The average diameter was ascertained to be 6.5 nm, with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm corresponded to the (100) crystal graphene face. Similarly, G-CDs also exhibited a uniform spherical morphology with a narrow particle size distribution ranging from 3 to 7 nm. The average particle size for G-CDs was found to be 5.13 nm, with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm consistent with the (100) crystal plane.

Further examination of the solid powder samples of B-CDs and G-CDs was conducted using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As illustrated in Figs. S1(a) and (b), B-CDs displayed a 22.1° peak, while G-CDs showed a peak at 22.5°, characteristic of the graphite (002) diffraction peak. This observation indicates the presence of graphene-like structures in both materials, with the interlayer spacing potentially affected by steric hindrance from edge functional groups or sp3 carbon on the graphene plane.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was utilized to analyze the surface functional groups present in of B-CDs and G-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1(c), both kinds of materials presented similar functional groups, with G-CDs showing slightly more pronounced absorption peaks compared to B-CDs. The analysis revealed the presence of OH, CCCO, and CO functional groups in both B-CDs and G-CDs, contributing to their hydrophilicity. The hydroxyl groups (OH) on their surfaces exhibited a broad absorption peak at 3430 cm−1 due to hydrogen bonding. Additionally, the observed characteristic absorption peaks of CO was 1100 cm−1, while the CO peak was 1650 cm−1, indicating the presence of carboxylic acid groups. The peak corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl groups on G-CDs shifted towards lower wavenumbers, potentially influenced by hydrogen bonding and aromatic ring conjugation effects.

Moreover, an absorption peak at 800 cm−1 was associated with the out-of-plane deformation vibration of NH, and a notable peak possibly associated with the stretching vibration of oxygen-sulfur bonds (O-S) was detected in G-CDs. These FT-IR spectral findings verified the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl functionalities on the surfaces of both B-CDs and G-CDs, enabling their conjugation with PCT monoclonal antibodies post-activation.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum presented in Fig. 1(d) demonstrated the presence of a CCCO conjugated structure on the surfaces of both B-CDs and G-CDs, resulting in a significant absorption band at 230 nm arising from π → π* transitions. In addition, B-CDs displayed a 347 nm absorption band, attributed to the n → π* transition of the carbonyl group (CO). Upon 347 nm excitation, the emission spectrum of B-CDs reached its 440 nm maximum fluorescence intensity. In contrast, G-CDs, influenced by the heteroatom sulfur, displayed a less intense absorption band at 410 nm contributed to the n → π* transition. The absorption peak of G-CDs at 460 nm was close to its excitation wavelength. Both groups showed weak absorption peaks in the 400–500 nm region.

Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were recorder to confirm the substantial overlap between the spectra of the two carbon dots, meeting the requirement for FRET occurrence. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) displayed the emission spectra of these across different excitation wavelengths (350–400 nm for B-CDs and 440–480 nm for G-CDs). B-CDs demonstrated intense blue luminescence within the spectral range of 400–500 nm, with the optimal emission peak was achieved at 442 nm under 370 nm excitation. Likewise, G-CDs exhibited strong green luminescence in the 500–600 nm region, achieving the highest emission peak at 522 nm when excited at a wavelength of 450 nm. The emission spectra of both CDs types appeared to be relatively insensitive to the excitation wavelength, possibly attributable to a single transition pattern near the band gap edge.

Fig. 2(c) demonstrates a significant spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of B-CDs and the excitation spectrum of G-CDs. The peak emission wavelength of B-CDs is determined to be 442 nm, whereas the optimal excitation wavelength for G-CDs is identified as 449 nm. According to these findings, the spatial distance between the two is less than 10 nm, promoting efficient FRET. Furthermore, Fig. 2(d) illustrates that under equivalent conditions, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is comparable to that of G-CDs. The fluorescence emission wavelengths of B-CDs and G-CDs in PBS solution remained unaffected, and the corresponding emission wavelength spectra are presented in Figs. S2 and S3.

As shown in Table 1 , the quantum yields of B-CDs and G-CDs were found to be comparable in water and exhibited a slight increase in PBS solution, thereby enhancing the feasibility of the subsequent detection process. To investigate the stability of B-CDs and G-CDs, they were dissolved in different solvents, and their emission spectra were analyzed. It was found that although the emission wavelength of the solutions remained consistent across different solvents, the fluorescence intensity exhibited minor variations depending on the solvent employed. These findings suggested that the close spatial arrangement and complementary spectral properties of these two groups enable effective resonance energy transfer. The interface between B-CDs and G-CDs for testing fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield is illustrated in Figs. S4–S11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pH in B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectra of B-CDs were obtained at 442 nm within the pH range of 6.0–8.0. As depicted in Fig. 3 (b) and (e), the emission spectrum gradually shifted to the red and the fluorescence intensity decreased as the pH value declined. Under UV irradiation, the B-CDs gradually changed to blue-green, and the fluorescence emission spectrum was redshifted from 442 nm to 479 nm. In contrast, the emission peak of G-CDs remained consistently at 522 nm and was unaffected by the pH of the solution.

The fluorescence characteristics of B-CDs and G-CDs did not change regularly in the pH solution. Specifically, B-CDs was most pronounced at pH values of 6.0 and 7.0, while G-CDs showed maximal fluorescence intensity at pH 4.0 and 5.0. It is worth noting that the optimal pH range for antigen–antibody protein interactions typically falls between 6.0 and 8.0 as reported in the literature [37,38]. Despite the red shift observed in the fluorescence of B-CDs at pH < 6.0, their functionality within the probe-based immune system remained unaffected.

The classical NHS/EDC coupling method was commonly used to conjugate antibodies to carbon dots. EDC reacted with carboxyl groups on the carbon dot surface to form an unstable amine-reactive O-acy isourea intermediate, which subsequently formed stable amide bonds with amino groups. However, these intermediates were highly unstable in aqueous solutions and can hydrolyze, regenerating carboxyl groups if they do not react with amines. Therefore, the combination of NHS and EDC enabled the conversion of the hydrolytic carboxyl group into a stable sulfur-NHS ester.

The donor (B-CDs) and acceptor (G-CDs) were conjugated with the corresponding capturing antibody (Ab1) and detecting antibody (Ab2) to form the donor probes B-CDs-Ab1 and acceptor probes G-CDs-Ab2. The successful connection of the PCT monoclonal antibody to the carbon site was verified through the establishment of a new amide bond within the probe. As shown in Fig. 4 (c), infrared spectra demonstrated significant distinctions between B-CDs-Ab1 and unaltered B-CDs. After antibody binding, the absorption peak of the hydroxyl group on B-CDs at 3432.99 cm−1 was enhanced and shifted to shorter wavelengths. Additional peaks in the 2970–2800 cm−1 range, corresponding to alkane CH bond stretching vibrations, were observed. Notable changes were also noted in the 1800–1000 cm−1 region. In Fig. S4 strong absorption peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 were evident for B-CDs-Ab1, representing the amide I and amide II bands, respectively. These bands (1700–1600 cm−1 and 1600–1500 cm−1) were sensitive indicators of protein secondary structure, confirming the formation of new amide bonds between B-CDs and antibody Ab1.

Additionally, the broad C-O bond peak of B-CDs at 1100 cm−1 exhibited varying peak intensities resulting from stretching vibrations during the coupling process (Fig. S12). The absorption peak in the 1470–1380 cm−1 region corresponded to alkane CH2 CH3 groups, while the fingerprint region reflected CC skeleton vibrations. TEM images (Fig. 4(a), (b)) showed a dense distribution of CDs on the antibody surfaces of B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2.

Fig. 5 (a) showed the UV–VIS absorption spectra of these B-CDs, Ab1, and B-CDs-Ab1 groups. B-CDs and G-CDs exhibited distinct absorption peaks at 230 nm and 350 nm, while Ab1 and Ab2 displayed characteristic antibody protein peaks at 280 nm. Following the coupling, both the 230 nm characteristic peak and the 260 nm absorption peak are observable in the case of B-CDs-Ab1. The blue shift in the characteristic peak at 280 nm may be influenced by the solvent effect. In addition, B-CDs-Ab1 exhibited a subtle absorption peak around 350 nm akin to B-CDs. Similarly, as depicted in Fig. 5(a) and 5(b), both B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 retained the weak antibody peak at 450 nm and the absorption peak of carbon dots in the wavelength range of 450–600 nm. This observation confirms the successful cross-linking of carbon dots with the antibody.

Fig. 5(c) showcased the B-CDs emission spectra, Ab1, and B-CDs-Ab1. B-CDs presented a 442 nm emission peaks, while Ab1 displayed weak peaks at 310 nm. B-CDs-Ab1 not only maintained the weak emission peak of the antibody at 310 nm, but also combined with the strong emission peak of B-CDs at 442 nm, suggesting the successful coupling of B-CDs to the PCT capturing antibody to form the donor probes. The G-CDs-Ab2 emission spectrum in Fig. 5(d) closely resembled that of B-CDs-Ab1, with absorption peaks at 520 nm post-coupling. Additionally, protein gel electrophoresis confirmed the successful conjugation of the antibody with its respective carbon dots (details in Fig. S13). These results collectively validated the successful conjugation of the antibody with carbon dots, affirming their suitability as probe materials for subsequent immunoassay experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of procalcitonin (PCT) in the PBS solution
ce:section.ce:para: A probe for immune sensing designed to detect PCT was developed, as illustrated in Fig. 2(c). The emission spectrum of B-CDs demonstrates a significant level of spectral overlap with the excitation spectrum of G-CDs. The excitation spectra for both B-CDs and G-CDs across different emission wavelengths were analyzed (refer to Figs. S2 and S3). According to the FRET principle, a bimolecular process where the excited state energy of a fluorophore (donor) was non-radiatively transferred to a neighboring ground-state molecule (acceptor) via weak dipole–dipole coupling. This process leads to donor fluorescence quenching and acceptor fluorescence excitation.

In this system, B-CDs-Ab1 functions as the energy donor probe and G-CDs-Ab2 serve as the acceptor probe, both conjugated to PCT antibodies to form immune probes. Upon antigen binding, the dual antibody probe rapidly attached to the antigen, initiating FRET. In this mechanism, the blue carbon dots transferred energy efficiently to the green carbon dots, resulting in a reducing of fluorescence in the blue carbon dots, while either enhancing or maintaining the fluorescence of the green carbon dots. The validation of FRET process within the immune system involved examining the changes in fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 before and after the introduction of the PCT antigen. In Fig. 6 (e) and (f), the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs-Ab1 at the emission wavelength of 440 nm was measured to be 13.07 ns in the absence of PCT antigen. Upon addition of the antigen, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor probe decreased slightly decreased to 12.71 ns. Correspondingly, at an emission wavelength of 520 nm, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs-Ab2 was determined to be 5.44 ns in the absence of antigen and increased to 6.24 ns following the addition of antigen. Despite the modest numerical alteration, this observation serves as evidence of the taking place of the FRET process between the donor and acceptor.

The B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 were combined with varying concentrations of PCT antigen, and the detection system was assessed to establish the correlation between the ratio of green fluorescence intensity to blue fluorescence intensity and PCT concentration. Upon the introduction of PCT, energy transfer from B-CDs to G-CDs was observed. As the PCT concentration increased, a notable reduction in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was recorded (from 446 to 294), while the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs varied between 116 and 126.8. The emission spectra for B-CDs-Ab1 and G-CDs-Ab2 were presented in Fig. 5(a) and Fig. S14, respectively.

It was noteworthy that the interaction between antibody and antigen-induced structural changes in the antibody protein, leads to a shift towards shorter wavelengths in the in peak emission and reduced fluorescence intensit. In PBS buffer solution, the linear equation for 0 to 5 ng/mL concentrations was F A /F D = −0.0045 × c [PCT]^2 + 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.3232, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 99.37 % and the LOD based on 3σ K − 1 was 0.1 ng m L −1 [39–41]. For concentrations ranging from 5 to 80 ng/mL, F A /F D = 0.0021 × c [PCT] + 0.4212 with a linear equation showed a correlation coefficient (R2) of 99.41 %. These results demonstrated a strong correlation between FA/FD and PCT concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference test of serum substances on the detection of PCT
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum contains various trace elements, proteins, amino acids, and other components. To verify the objectivity of the fluorescence immunoassay in serum, substances such as 0.1 M copper ion, iron ion, human serum albumin, magnesium ion, zinc ion, potassium ion, manganese ion, cysteine, glutamic acid, glucose, tryptophan, etc., were introduced into the donor probe and recipient probe solutions. The interference degree of each substance on the donor and recipient probes was judged by the corresponding changes in fluorescence value. As depicted in Fig. 3(e), these substances exhibited minimal interference with the fluorescence intensity of both the donor and acceptor probes. Therefore, we conclude that this method is suitable for serum PCT detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of low-concentration PCT in blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: Following the verification of the probe’s efficacy in the detecting PCT in PBS buffers, its applicability in blood samples was investigated. Detection sensitivity was highest for low concentrations of PCT (0 to 5 ng/mL) in serum, despite the presence of various endogenous fluorescent substances in blood that could potentially obscure the fluorescence signals emitted by CDs. To address this issue, serum samples were diluted by various factors. As shown in Figs. S18 and S19, even at a 400-fold dilution, the fluorescence signals from both B-CDs and G-CDs remained strong and unaffected.

Subsequent tests were conducted using a 400-fold dilution of serum, yielding results consistent with those observed in PBS buffer. Compared to PBS buffer, the serum exhibits lower transparency, potentially causing internal filtering effects where light absorption or scattering by serum components decreases the fluorescence emission ratio of the dual-probe system. The fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs-Ab1 probe emission spectrum diminished with rising concentrations of the PCT antigen in serum. Fig. 5(d) depicted a robust linear relationship between PCT concentration (0.5–5 ng/mL) and the fluorescence intensity ratio. The linear regression equation, F A /F D = −0.0015 × c [PCT]^2 + 0.0154 × c [PCT] + 0.2368, achieved a high regression coefficient of 99.91 % and the LOD based on 3σ K − 1 was 0.26 ng m L−1 [39–41].

In summary, the immunoassay techniques employing carbon dot-labeled probes provide a rapid and efficient way to detect PCT levels. This approach leverages carbon dots and fluorescently labeled probes targeting PCT antigens, facilitating swift and consistent immunoassays. The fluorescence intensity ratio F A /F D in PBS buffer exhibits a linear relationship with PCT concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 80 ng/mL. Similarly, in diluted human serum, the linear relationship is observed for low PCT concentrations (0.5–5 ng/mL). Compared to alternative methods, this technique stands out for its convenience and efficiency. It involves directly mixing PCT with two types of immune probes for rapid detection. This system holds potential for the creation of a sensitive detection platform for disease markers through the utilization of the fluorescence properties of carbon dots. Future research will prioritize enhancing the stability and precision of this approach to meet the demands of diagnostic initiatives requiring increased sensitivity and lowered detection limits.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFB3210300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82172077, 22201298 and 62027825), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), the Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200038 and YJKYYQ20210032), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126207.

ce:section.ce:para:
